journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of Family Medicine and Primary Care

title-group.article-title: Prevalence and risk factors for post-COVID-19 syndrome on medical students in Tabuk, Saudi Arabia-2023-2024

aff.italic: Department of Medical Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, University of Tabuk, Tabuk, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Department of Internal Medicine and Endocrine, Faculty of Medicine, University of Tabuk, Tabuk, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Department of Family and Community Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, University of Tabuk, Tabuk, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Department of Internal Medicine, University of Tabuk, Tabuk, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Department of Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Northern Border University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

author-notes.corresp: Address for correspondence: Prof. Hyder Mirghani, Department of Internal Medicine, University of Tabuk, Tabuk - 51941, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. E-mail: s.hyder63#hotmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright: © 2025 Journal of Family Medicine and Primary Care

license.license-p: This is an open access journal, and articles are distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 License, which allows others to remix, tweak, and build upon the work non-commercially, as long as appropriate credit is given and the new creations are licensed under the identical terms.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: COVID-19 has infected 221 million individuals worldwide and new strains are discovered continuously. An important complication is the post-COVID-19 syndrome that affects various organs with substantial morbidities. Studies on post-COVID-19 symptoms and risk factors are scarce. We aimed to assess the same among medical students at the University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia.

sec.title: Methodology:
sec.p: This cross-sectional study was conducted among 424 medical students at the University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia, during the period from July 2023 to October 2024. A web-based questionnaire detailing the demographic factors, post-COVID-19 symptoms, comorbidities, vitamin D deficiency, and the cumulative grade average (GPA) was used.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: Out of the 424 students, 38.2% were overweight and 9.4% were obese, 26.9% had bronchial asthma, and diabetes was found in 11.8%. Smoking was observed in 25%, and 58.5% were vitamin D deficient, while 22.6% were hospitalized with COVID-19. The majority of students received > two doses (74.1%), 24.5% received two doses, and 1.4% received one dose. The majority (80.7%) of students reported at least one post-COVID-19 symptom, and headache was the commonest symptom (63.7%), followed by cough in 50.5%. The absence from classrooms was 54.2%, 32.1%, and 13.7% (3 days, 3-8 days, and >10 days/month, respectively), and 44.8% thought that long COVID-19 negatively affected their GPA.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: Post-COVID-19 syndrome was associated with diabetes, bronchial asthma, and vitamin D deficiency, odd ratios, 95% CI, 0.011-0.910, 0.005-0.312, 0.203-0.821, respectively, P values > 0.05. No significant association was found regarding, age, gender, and smoking, P values >0.05.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: COVID-19 affected 221 million worldwide (September 2021 data (https://www.worldometers.info/coronavirus/). Therefore, 66,000,000 people could suffer the long-term consequences of COVID-19 (post-COVID-19 syndrome), with vaccination as the only problem. Long-COVID-19 syndrome is a great public health challenge of gargantuan proportions.[1] COVID-19 disease is an acute infection, but with long-term multi-organ involvement.[2]
Although most patients with acute COVID-19 recover, however, some might have continuous symptoms for 12 weeks or longer (post-COVID-19 syndrome).[3]

There is no agreed definition of post-COVID-19 syndrome, and the symptoms that extend for more than twelve weeks after getting COVID-19 are the most used definition. In some, the symptoms might extend for years with deleterious consequences.[4]

The post-COVID-19 syndrome affects most organs, the central nervous system,[5] cardiovascular, renal system, and other systems. Therefore, the disease is not only confined to the respiratory system. Importantly, some might have symptoms similar to the acute coronavirus infection.[6]

Some previously healthy people who survived COVID-19 have been left involvement of the cardiovascular and kidney function. Whether the damage is permanent and if it affects their functional capacity is to be determined. Other people continue to have myalgia, fatigue, dyspnea, headache, brain fogs, sleep disturbances, and inability to exercise or even do their jobs or home activities (long haulers).[78]

Previous literature found that more than 50% of people who “recovered” from COVID-19 continue to have symptoms 12 weeks or more. Such lingering symptoms have been reported following “recovery” from several other infectious illnesses, including mononucleosis, Lyme disease, and SARS (another disease caused by a coronavirus). Therefore, it is not surprising that this illness—which is similar to the illness, called myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome—develops following COVID-19.[910]

COVID-19 disease is complex and involves other systems together with the pulmonary manifestations; therefore, the management needs to be continued after hospitals discharge. The current result will be used as a database for further research, acknowledging the risk factors with enable the prevention of this common disease.[3]

This project is unique in that only a few studies have assessed post-COVID-19 syndrome in the young age group, and a study conducted in the USA found that 51% of University students who contracted COVID-19 were classified with post-COVID-19 syndrome.[11] Another study found a prevalence of 18.5% of post-COVID-19 among adolescents.[12] The above results imply that post-COVID-19 is not a disease of the middle-aged and elderly. In addition, post-COVID-19 might affect the student’s mental health and academic performance. Therefore, we went ahead to propose this project. This study aims to assess the prevalence of post-COVID-19 syndrome and its associated factors among medical students in Tabuk, Saudi Arabia.

sec.title: Methodology
sec.p: This cross-sectional study was conducted among 424 medical students in the Medical College, University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia, during the period from October 2023 to January 2024. The students were enrolled by simple randomization from the student lists to complete the sample. A structured web-based questionnaire was used to collect the information. The questionnaire was distributed in English language and through Google Forms.

The sample size was calculated by Calculator. Net, with a marginal error of 5%, 95% CI, 95, and the total students in the University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia, were 798. Thus, the sample size stood at 385 students and was increased to 424 to minimize the error.

A structured web-based questionnaire was used to collect demographic factors including age, sex, cumulative grade average (GPA) during the current semester, vitamin D status, comorbid diseases, and history of hospitalization, in addition to symptoms of post-COVID-19. The questionnaire consists of general symptoms including tiredness or fatigue that interferes with daily life, symptoms that get worse after physical or mental effort, and fever. In addition to respiratory symptoms, there are neurological symptoms, gastrointestinal symptoms, rash, myalgia, and changes in menstrual cycles. The symptoms must be present for >12 weeks post-COVID-19 attack.

sec.title: Ethical consideration
sec.p: In the present study, we strictly followed the Declaration of Helsinki guidelines. The purpose of the survey was conveyed to the students, and they have been informed that their participation is voluntary. If the participants choose not to agree, then the questionnaire will automatically be closed.

The patient’s privacy was ensured, and name or identity number was recorded. The information was kept confidential and professional. All the information collected was used only for research. Ethical clearance was obtained from the ethical committee University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia (ref. UT-311-154-2023, dated, 02-10-2023).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: The Statistical Package for Social Sciences (SPSS) (IBM, version, 20, New York) was used for data analysis which was presented as percentages, mean, and standard deviation unless otherwise specified. Binary logistic regression was used to assess the relationship between post-COVID-19 and various student’s characteristics. A P value of <0.05 was considered significant.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: There were 424 medical students (49.5% women), and the majority of participants were in the age group from 21-23 years (43.4%) followed by 24-27 years (28.2%).

Regarding the body mass index, 38.2% were overweight and 9.4% were obese. Comorbidities in medical students were as follows: 26.9% had bronchial asthma, diabetes mellitus was found in 11.8%, smoking was observed in 25%, and 58.5% were vitamin D deficient, while 22.6% were hospitalized with COVID-19. Regarding COVID-19 vaccination, the majority of students received > two doses (74.1%), 24.5% received two doses, and 1.4 received one dose [Table 1].

caption.p: Basic characteristics of the study group

sec.p: Third-year students constituted 21.2%, followed by the fourth-year. The distribution in different classes is shown in Figure 1.

caption.p: The number of students in each class

sec.p: Regarding the frequency of post-COVID-19 symptoms (>12 weeks), 80.7% of students reported at least 1 symptom, 18.4% of students suffered from 1-3 post-COVID-19 symptoms, and 62.3% had > three symptoms for. Headache was the commonest symptom of post-COVID-19 (63.7%), followed by cough 50.5%, and abdominal pain [Table 2]. It depicted other COVID-19 that persisted for 12 weeks.

caption.p: Symptoms that persisted for at least 12 weeks among students with COVID-19

sec.p: In the present study, more than half of students (54.2%) were absent in three days a month, 32.1% were not reporting to the classroom for 3-8 days/per month, and 13.7% of students’ absences were > 10 days/per month. Regarding the effect on cumulative grade average, 44.8% thought that long COVID-19 negatively affected their GPA [Table 3].

caption.p: Absence from classrooms and effect on the cumulative grade average among medical students with COVID-19

tr.td: Absence from classrooms due to persistent COVID-19 symptoms
Effect on the Cumulative Grade Average

sec.p: The current study observed the association of post-COVID-19 syndrome diabetes, bronchial asthma, and vitamin D deficiency, 95% CI, 0.011-0.910, 0.005-0.312, 0.203-0.821, respectively, P values, 0.041, 0.002, and 0.012, respectively. No significant association was found regarding, age, gender, and smoking, 1.553-0.453, 0.419-1.968, and 0291-2.534, respectively [Table 4].

caption.p: The association between post-COVID-19 syndrome and various characteristics among medical students with COVID-19

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In the present study, headache was the most common symptom in line with a previous study that found headache was the most common symptom 6-9 months after COVID-19.[13] Regarding post-COVID-19 symptoms, 80.7% of patients had at least one post-COVID-19 symptom in line with a previous study published by Mahmoud et al.[14] in Saudi Arabia. Respiratory, musculoskeletal, and gastrointestinal symptoms were similar in the Saudi study and the current results. Asthenia was reported in 27.4% similar to the findings of Michelen and colleagues[15] and Iqbal et al.[16] who reported fatigue in 31.1% and 37%, respectively. Dysgeusia was found in 25.9% of students and is similar to the findings of Maamar et al.[17] However, anosmia was found in 26.9% which was lower than Maamar et al.[17] findings. Another study included 200 patients and found similar results regarding anosmia.[18] The authors found no association between gustatory, olfactory symptoms, and age, in line with the current observation. In the present study, 98.6% of students had two or more doses of COVID-19 vaccine. No association was found between the symptoms prevalence and the COVID-19 vaccine. The current findings were in line with a study published in the UK.[19] Further studies reported partial[20] and complete[21] protection against COVID-19. In the present study, no association was found between post-COVID-19 syndrome, age, sex, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and active neoplasia in contradiction to previous studies that found an association.[2223] Plausible explanations were that our sample was younger and lies in the same age group, in addition to and different sample sizes of the different studies. The current data showed an association between diabetes mellitus and long-COVID-19 syndrome, similar to previous studies that concluded the bidirectional relationship between diabetes mellitus and post-COVID-19 syndrome.[24] We found an association between vitamin D deficiency and post-COVID-19 in agreement with di Filippo et al. and Hikmet et al. findings.[2526] The current findings were in contradiction to a previous study that found post-COVID-19 symptoms including fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance were independent of vitamin D status.[27] Previous studies showed that vitamin D supplementation was not effective in long-COVID-19 treatment; the authors explained their findings by the downregulation of vitamin D receptors by the SARS-CoV-2 virus.[2829] Our findings were similar to Sudre and colleagues who conducted a study in the UK and concluded the association between long-COVID-19 and bronchial asthma.[22] We found no association between smoking and post-COVID-19 syndrome in agreement with Jones et al.[30] who found no association. However, our findings contradict the findings of two previous studies.[3132] Importantly, nearly half of the students were absent for more than three days/month and thought that the post-COVID-19 symptoms negatively impacted their cumulative grade average. Increasing the awareness of long-COVID-19 syndrome and its risk factors is vital for early detection and treatment. A recent study found the association with allergic diseases and proposed antihistamines as a future treatment.

The current study’s limitations are that the study was conducted at a single college, the cross-sectional nature of the study, and the reliance on a self-administered questionnaire that is more prone to subjectivity.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The study found that the majority of the students had at least one post-COVID-19 symptom; diabetes mellitus, bronchial asthma, and vitamin D deficiency were associated with long-COVID-19 syndrome. No associations were evident regarding age, sex, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and smoking. Further multi-center studies investigating the effects of vitamin D supplementations, improving good glycemic control, and bronchial asthma control on the long-COVID-19 are recommended.

Limitation: The current study’s limitations are that the study was conducted at a single college, the cross-sectional nature of the study, and the reliance on a self-administered questionnaire that is more prone to subjectivity.

sec.title: Ethical consideration
sec.p: Ethical clearance was obtained from the ethical committee University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia (ref. UT-311-154-2023, dated, 02-10-2023).

sec.title: Availability of data and material
sec.p: Data are available within submitted article.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: There are no conflicts of interest.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: We would like to acknowledge Dr Sawsan Albalawi, Associate Prof, of Pediatrics and Hematology, University of Tabuk, Saudi Arabia, for her assistance during the whole manuscript.
